planting.note: Plant container-grown transplants or rooted cuttings in spring or fall in full sun to full shade in any well-drained to moderately wet soil; yaupon is one of the most adaptable native shrubs for the Southeast and Gulf Coast, tolerating drought, flooding, salt spray, clay, and deep shade.

directions: Plant container-grown transplants or rooted cuttings in spring or fall in full sun to deep shade in virtually any soil from dry sand to heavy clay to seasonally flooded sites; yaupon holly is arguably the most adaptable native shrub in the Southeast, tolerating conditions that eliminate every other choice. Female plants produce masses of tiny, brilliant-red berries that provide critical winter food for mockingbirds, robins, hermit thrushes, and cedar waxwings. The caffeine-containing leaves were historically brewed by indigenous peoples as the 'black drink' used in ceremonial purification — yaupon is the only caffeinated plant native to North America, and interest in it as an herbal tea is growing. Compact cultivars like 'Nana' and 'Schilling's Dwarf' stay under 3 feet and make excellent low hedges; tall forms are used for privacy screens, specimen trees, and topiary.

planting.note: Plant container-grown specimens in spring in moist to wet, acidic, humus-rich soil in full sun to partial shade; sweetbay magnolia is one of the finest trees for moist, poorly drained, or seasonally flooded sites in zone 5–10 gardens.

directions: Plant container-grown specimens in spring in moist to wet, acidic, humus-rich soil in full sun to partial shade; sweetbay magnolia is a graceful native tree or multi-stemmed large shrub of eastern coastal plain swamps and streambanks, one of the few truly large shade trees that thrives in consistently wet, poorly drained, acidic soil. The creamy-white, lemon-scented flowers appear in spring and continue sporadically through summer, filling the surrounding air with fragrance; the elliptic leaves are dark green above and silver-white beneath, creating a shimmering effect in the breeze. In zone 5–7 it is deciduous to semi-deciduous; in zone 8–10 it is essentially evergreen. The red-orange seeds attract migrating songbirds in fall and winter. An outstanding choice for wet garden sites, rain gardens, and streamside plantings where conventional trees would not survive.

planting.note: Plant container-grown transplants or rooted cuttings in spring or fall in partial shade to full sun in moist, slightly acidic, well-drained to moderately wet soil; Virginia sweetspire is highly adaptable and one of the most rewarding native shrubs for shaded, moist, or difficult sites.

directions: Plant container-grown transplants or rooted cuttings in spring or fall in partial shade to full sun in moist, slightly acidic, well-drained to moderately wet soil; Virginia sweetspire is a superb three-season native shrub producing fragrant white flower racemes in early summer, exceptional burgundy-red autumn foliage color (among the best of any native shrub), and attractive arching winter stems. It tolerates more shade than most flowering shrubs, making it one of the best options for the partially shaded mixed border; it also tolerates seasonally moist and wet conditions near streams and rain gardens. The cultivar 'Henry's Garnet' is a superior compact selection with especially vivid fall color, reaching 3–4 feet. Spreads slowly by suckers to form a naturalistic thicket that is attractive in native plantings and wildlife gardens.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings or container plants in spring or fall in well-drained, moderately fertile soil in full sun to partial shade against a sturdy trellis, arbor, fence, or tree; crossvine is a vigorous native climber that attaches by forked tendrils with adhesive discs.

directions: Plant rooted cuttings or container plants in spring or fall against a sturdy trellis, arbor, fence, or mature tree in well-drained, moderately fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; crossvine is a vigorous native evergreen vine of the Southeast that produces masses of large, trumpet-shaped, orange-red to yellow flowers in mid-spring that are a critical early hummingbird nectar source just as ruby-throated hummingbirds return to the Southeast from their Caribbean winter. The bicolor flowers (orange-red outside, yellow inside in the species; brick-red in the cultivar 'Tangerine Beauty') cover the mature vine in late April to May in a stunning display. It climbs by adhesive tendrils to 30–50 feet and is semi-evergreen in zone 6–7, fully evergreen in zone 8–9. Once established it is drought-tolerant and self-supporting on any surface it can grip.

planting.note: Plant bulbs 2–3 inches deep and 4–6 inches apart in spring in well-drained, moderately fertile soil in full sun; rain lilies naturalize freely in lawns and low-maintenance landscapes in zone 7–11 and multiply rapidly by offsets.

directions: Plant bulbs 2–3 inches deep and 4–6 inches apart in spring in well-drained, moderately fertile soil in full sun; rain lily earns its name by blooming within days of summer rainstorms, producing clusters of small, white, crocus-like flowers with golden stamens that seem to appear magically from bare, grassy foliage after each significant rainfall in summer and fall. The narrow, dark-green, rush-like leaves are attractive year-round in zone 7–11, forming spreading colonies as the bulbs offset freely. Plant in masses in lawns, borders, and naturalizing areas — it is low-maintenance once established, needing no irrigation beyond natural rainfall. In zone 7–8 mulch heavily in winter to protect the bulbs; in zone 9–11 it is essentially evergreen and fully self-sustaining. The related pink-flowered Zephyranthes rosea and yellow Z. citrina are equally easy and can be combined for a multicolor rain-lily display.

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 4–6 weeks before last frost at 70–75°F or take stem cuttings from overwintered plants; transplant after all frost danger has passed in full sun to partial shade in fertile, well-drained soil. In zone 9–12 plant container specimens directly into the landscape.

directions: Start seeds indoors 4–6 weeks before last frost or root stem cuttings from overwintered plants, then transplant after all frost danger has passed in full sun to partial shade in fertile, well-drained soil; cigar plant is a compact, free-flowering tropical with striking narrow tubular flowers in bright red tipped in dark purple and white — strongly resembling tiny lit cigars, complete with a 'ash' tip — that hummingbirds visit continuously from spring through frost. It blooms without pause throughout the summer heat, making it exceptional for containers, borders, and hanging baskets where continuous color is needed. In zone 9–12 it is a perennial sub-shrub; in other climates treat as a warm-season annual or overwinter cuttings indoors. The related Cuphea hyssopifolia (false heather) is smaller with tiny lavender flowers and equally heat-tolerant.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Scarify or nick the hard seed coat and soak overnight before sowing indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant to full sun in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile soil after last frost. In zone 9–11 it is semi-evergreen and may be planted year-round.

directions: Nick the hard seed coat, soak seeds overnight, then start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost in well-drained potting mix at 70–80°F; transplant after last frost in full sun in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile soil in zone 9–11. Butterfly senna produces masses of bright golden-yellow flowers from fall through early winter on arching branches with compound, ferny foliage — one of the showiest fall-blooming shrubs for Gulf Coast and South Florida landscapes. It is a critical host plant for cloudless sulphur butterfly larvae, and the adult butterflies nectar heavily on the bright flowers. Growth is fast and may require pruning to maintain shape; cut back hard in early spring before new growth begins to keep the plant compact. The related Senna alata (candlestick plant) blooms in candle-like spikes and is equally effective in zone 9–11.

planting.note: Plant container-grown transplants in spring or fall in moist, slightly acidic, well-drained to moderately wet soil in full sun to partial shade; possumhaw requires a male pollinator nearby for berry production — one male plant will pollinate up to three female plants.

directions: Plant container-grown transplants in spring or fall in moist, slightly acidic, well-drained to moderately moist soil in full sun to partial shade; possumhaw holly is a multi-season native shrub that blooms with small white flowers in spring attractive to native bees, then develops abundant bright-orange to red berries that ripen in fall and persist through winter to provide critical food for cedar waxwings, mockingbirds, bluebirds, and other frugivorous birds. A male pollinator must be planted nearby for fruiting; nurseries often sell named female selections (Warren's Red, Council Fire) paired with a male. Tolerates the periodic flooding and clay soils common to Gulf Coast lowland sites. Exceptional as a wildlife garden shrub, in rain gardens, and along creek margins.

planting.note: Scarify seeds and direct-sow in fall in full sun in deep, well-drained, lean to moderately fertile soil; germination is improved by cold-moist stratification. Once established, false blue indigo forms a long-lived, deep-rooted clump that should not be disturbed or moved.

directions: Scarify seeds by nicking or rubbing with sandpaper, then direct-sow in fall in deep, well-drained, lean to moderately fertile soil in full sun; false blue indigo is slow to establish but extremely long-lived once rooted — it may take 2–3 years to reach blooming size but plants can survive in the same spot for decades without needing division. The blue-green clover-like foliage is attractive all season; in late spring dramatic spikes of indigo-blue pea-like flowers appear, followed by inflated black seed pods that are decorative through winter and rattle in the breeze. A deep taproot makes it impossible to transplant once established, so siting is permanent. Outstanding as a drought-tolerant native prairie perennial; it is also a host plant for Wild Indigo Duskywing, Hoary Edge, and Orange Sulphur butterfly larvae.
